results.text: COMPLETE PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS
BENJAMIN MCKAY
ABsTrAcT. The first examples of complete projective connections are uncov-
ered: normal projective connections on surfaces whose geodesics are all closed
and embedded are complete, as are normal projective connections induced
from complete affine connections with slowly decaying positive Ricci curva-
ture. Moreover, I catalogue all of the projective connections on curves, and
explain the relationship between projective and affine connections.
CoNTENTS
Introduction
Structure equations of projective connections
Classification of projective connections on curves
Vector bundles and descent data
Geodesics
Positive Ricci curvature
Jacobi vector fields
Normal projective connections on surfaces
Tameness
10. Affine connections, projective connections, projective structures and
normal projective connections
Conclusions
References
1. INTrodUCtiON
The central idea of this article is that completeness of projective connections is
subtle, even on compact manifolds, and there seems to be no easy way to decide
whether a projective connection is complete. In my recent work [14], I discovered
that complete complex projective connections are flat, and I decided to look for
complete real projective connections which are not flat. I was surprised to find that
none were known; in this article you will find the first examples.
Definitions are presented later, but for the moment recall that every Riemann-
ian manifold has a distinguished projective connection, and that this imposes on
every geodesic a natural choice of parameterization, well defined modulo projective
transformations. This parameterization is not the arc length parameterization, in
many examples, but it is unchanged if we change metric, as long as we keep the
same geodesics. For example, in real projective spaces Pn, the arc length param-
eterization is the natural parameterization. But therefore in affine space, thought
Date: February 8, 2020.
BENJAMIN MCKAY
of as an affine chart of Pn (which gives it the usual straight lines as geodesics),
following the projective parameterization can run us off to infinity in finite time.
This is the bizarre incompleteness of affine space. Worse, since a flat torus is a
quotient of affine space, it is also incomplete! Indeed, the only complete exam-
ples known (before the results below) were the sphere and projective space (with
standard metrics).
The concept of completeness of Cartan geometries is subtle, raised explicitly
for the first time by Ehresmann [6] (also see Kobayashi [8], Kobayashi & Nagano
[11], Clifton [5], Bates [1]), and plays a central role in Sharpeâs book [16], but is
also clearly visible beneath the surface in numerous works of Cartan. Roughly
speaking, completeness concerns the ability to compare a geometry to some notion
of flat geometry, by rolling along curves. There were no examples of complete
projective connections except for the sphere and projective space (which are both
flat) until now:
Theorem 1. Every normal projective connection on a surface, all of whose geodesics
are closed embedded curves, is complete.
Theorem 2. Every complete torsion-free affine connection with positive Ricci cur-
vature decaying slower than quadratically induces a complete normal projective con-
nection.
Analysis of projective connections is notoriously difficult because the structure
group is not compact, a kind of inherent slipperiness. The antithesis of incom-
pleteness is known as projective hyperbolicity; see Kobayashi [9] and Wu [17] for
examples of projective hyperbolicity.
Ãlie Cartan [3] introduced the notion of projective connection; Kobayashi &
Nagano [11], Gunning [7] and Borel [2] provide a contemporary review; we will use
the definitions of Kobayashi & Nagano.
2. STRUCTURE EQUATIONS OF PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS

Lemma 1. There are uniquely determined functions Kjk, Kijk, Kjkl (called the
curvature functions) so that the structure equations of Cartan in table 1 are satisfied.
Proof. This requires elementary applications of Cartan's lemma; see Kobayashi &
Nagano [11] for proof.
Definition 2. A projective connection is called flat if the curvature functions van-
ish.
Example 1. The model of a projective connection is the one on Pn given by taking
E = G, Î© = gâ1 dg the left invariant MaurerâCartan 1-form, and the map g
P GL (pn) â g [e0]  Pn. The model is flat.
Lemma 2. A projective connection is flat just when it is locally (i.e. on open
subsets of M ) isomorphic to the model.
Proof. Take the differential system Î© âgâ1 dg = 0 on E Ã P GL (Pn). It satisfies the
conditions of the Frobenius theorem just when the curvature functions vanish. The
g orbits are Cauchy characteristics, so maximal connected integal manifolds are
BENJAMIN MCKAY
unions of these orbits. The group G0 has finitely many path components, and the
union of finitely many integral manifolds is an integral manifold, so each connected
integral manifold is contained in a unique G0-invariant and g-invariant integral
manifold. Integral manifolds are the graphs of local isomorphisms, which descend
to maps M â Pnby G0-equivariance.
Example 2. The sphere Sn has a 2-1 covering map Sn â pn. Pulling back
the bundle from the model, and the 1-form gâ1 dg from the model, we find a flat
projective connection on Sn.
Definition 3 (Ehresmann [6]). A projective connection is complete when the vector
fields Ã are all complete (i.e their flows are defined for all time).
Example 3. The vector fields Ä on the model generate the right action of P GL (Pn)
on itself, and therefore the model is complete.
Example 4. A covering space of a complete projective connection is complete,
because the relevant vector fields are pullbacks under covering maps. Therefore Sn
is complete.
Definition 4. A isomorphism of projective connections Ej â Mj (j = 0, 1) is a
G-equivariant diffeomorphism Î¦ : E0 â E1 preserving the projective connection
forms: Î¦*Î©1 = Î©0. An infinitesimal symmetry of a projective connection E â M
is a vector field X on E commuting with the G0-action, and satisfying LxÎ© = 0.
Lemma 3. If a projective connection is complete then every infinitesimal symmetry
is a complete vector field.
Proof. The vector fields A commute with X, so they permute the flow lines of X
around in all directions. Therefore the time for which the flow of X is defined
is locally constant. But then it cannot diminish as we move along a flow line.
Therefore the flow of X is defined for all time.
Lemma 4. A projective connection E â M is flat just when the infinitesimal sym-
metries act locally transitively on E, complete and flat just when the automorphism
group is transitive on E.
Proof. This forces invariance of the curvature, which therefore must be constant.
The curvature is equivariant under the G0 action, so lives in a G0-representation.
One can easily see that there are no nonzero G0-invariant vectors in that represen-
tation.
Theorem 3. Every flat projective connection E â M is obtained by taking the uni-
versal covering space M â M, mapping M â pn by a local diffeomorphism, pulling
back the model projective connection, and taking the quotient projective connection
via some morphism Ï1(M ) â P GL (pn).
Proof. Without loss of generality, assume that M is connected. Put the differential
system Î© = gâ1 dg on the manifold E Ã P GL (Pn). By the Frobenius theorem, the
manifold is foliated by leaves (maximal connected integral manifolds). Because the
system is invariant under left action of P GL (Pn) on itself, this action permutes
leaves. Define vector fields A on E Ã P GL (Pn) by adding the one from E with the
one (by the same name) from P GL (Pn). The flow of A on P GL (Pn) is defined
for all time, so the vector field A on E Ã P GL (Pn) has flow through a point (e, g)
COMPLETE PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS
defined for as long as the flow is defined down on E. These vector fields A are
Cauchy characteristics, so the leaves are invariant under their flows.
The group G0 has finitely many components, so the G0 orbit of a leaf is a
finite union of leaves. Let Î be a G0 orbit of a leaf. For two choices Î0 and Î1,
containing points (ei, gi)  Îi, after G0 action we can draw a path from e0 to e1
in E, consisting of finitely many flows of A vector fields, so such a path lifts to our
leaf. Therefore Î1 must contain a point (e0, g), so our choice of Î is unique up to
P GL (Pn)action.
The inclusion Î â E Ã P GL (Pn) defines two local diffeomorphisms Î â E and
Î â P GL (Pn), both A equivariant. Consider the first of these. Let F be a fiber of
Î â E over some point e  E. Define local coordinates on E by inverting the map
A  g â eAe  E near A = 0 (n.b. this map is only defined near A = 0 anyway,
and is a diffeomorphism in some neighborhood, say U, of 0). Then map
U Ã F â Î
by (A, f ) â eAf , clearly a local diffeomorphism. Therefore Î â E is a covering
map, and G0-equivariant, so covers a covering map M = Î/G0 â M = E/G0.
Thus Î â M is the pullback bundle of E â M.
The map Î â P GL (Pn) is G0-equivariant, so descends to a map M â Pn,
and by definition on Î we have Î© = gâ1 dg, so this map is pullback of projective
connections.
Corollary 1. A complete flat projective connection E â M with dim M â¥ 2 is
isomorphic to a quotient Sn/Î with Î â SO (() n + 1) a finite group.
Proof. The universal covering space has to be the same as for Pn, so Sn. But then
the quotient has to be by a discrete group Î with morphism Î â P GL (n + 1, R).
Since Sn is compact, it can only act as covering space of compact spaces, so with
a finite group Î of deck transformations. Every finite subgroup of GL (n + 1, R)
preserves a positive definite inner product on Rn+1, so Î sits in a conjugate of
PO(n+1). Thus, after isomorphism, Î â PO(n+1) a morphism of finite groups.
3. CLASSIFICATION OF PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS ON CURVES
Lemma 5. Every projective connection E â M on a curve M is flat.
Proof. Curvature is a semibasic 2-form, but M has only one dimension.
Theorem 4. Consider the model P1 = A âª â, A = R. We draw the universal
cover P1 â P1 as

BENJAMIN MCKAY
for any choice of angle 0 â¤ Î¸ < 2Ï. The projective connections on an open curve
(modulo isomorphism) are the following open subsets of P1:
elliptic

hyperbolic
(any number of copies of the affine line may be contained in these open parabolic
and hyperbolic curves). The hyperbolic curves are those with an invariant metric
(dz/z)Â² (and consequently an invariant affine connection: the Levi-Civita connec-
tion), while the parabolic are those with no invariant metric but have an invariant
affine connection, and the elliptic are those which are complete.
Proof. Take C a curve, and E â C a projective connection. By theorem 3 on page 4
the universal cover C â¡ R â C has a local diffeomorphism C â P1, from which the
projective connection is pulled back. So we have to consider all open subsets of P1,
up to the action of the fundamental group Ï1 (P1) and the action of the appropriate
covering group of P GL (P1). The fundamental group just shifts zeros 0 â 0, but
the covering group of P GL (P1) is locally 3 point transitive, so the classification
for open curves is elementary. For closed curves, we have to classify groups of
projective transformations acting on P1 which will induce a Hausdorff quotient on
the interval C, up to conjugacy by the projective transformations fixing C, which
is also elementary.

Proposition 1. TM = (E Ã Rn)/G0
Proof. Given a vector field X on M, the functions X â¢ : E â Rn are G0-equivariant,
so form a section of (E Ã Rn)/G0 â M . Clearly X = 0 just where that section van-

5. GEODESICS

Definition 7. We define a geodesic to be a curve of vanishing geodesic curvature.
Equivalently, geodesics are the curves on M which are the projections to M of
the integral manifolds EC  E of the differential system Ï1 = Î³1 = 0.
BENJAMIN MCKAY
Definition 8. The geodesic flow is the flow of the vector field dual to Ï1.
The flow lines of geodesic flow are contained in these integral manifolds; since
these flow lines are permuted by the action of G+, the manifolds Ec for C a geodesic
are precisely the G+-orbits of flow lines of geodesic flow.
Definition 9. Given a connected immersed curve t : C â M, and a chosen point
c0  C, we will roll an immersed curve C onto Pn to produce an immersion C â Pn
(called its development), as follows. Take the differential system Î© = gâ1 dg on

tells us that EC Ã P GL (Pn) is foliated by leaves (i.e. maximal connected integral

Take the G+ orbit of any leaf, say Î â EC Ã P GL (Pn), containing a point
(e, 1) with Ï(e) = c, and map Î â P GL (Pn) â Pn by the obvious maps. By G+ -
equivariance, this determines a map C â Pn, and changing the choice of Î doesn't
change the map C â pn.
Definition 10. Take an immersed curve t : C â M and a projective connection
E â M. Let N+  G+ be the subgroup of G+ acting trivially on P1 â Pn. Then
EC = Ec/N+ â C is a principal right G0-bundle, where G0 = G+/N+. Let
G = G/N+. The 1-form Î© = Î© mod n+  Î©1 (Ec)   is a projective connection
on EC â C. Call this the induced projective connection on C.
Lemma 7. C is a geodesic just when
(1) the development C â Pn maps C â P1  pn, and
(2) the induced projective connection is the pullback via C â P1.
Proof. This is immediate from the structure equations.
It is elementary to prove:
Theorem 5 (Kobayashi [8]). Let M be a manifold with projective connection.
Every immersed curve in projective space is the development of a curve in M just
when M is complete.
Definition 11. A curve C in a manifold M with projective connection is complete
when the induced projective connection Ec is complete.
Lemma 8. A projective connection is complete just when all of its geodesics are
complete.
Proof. The geodesic flow is tangent to all of the manifolds Ec for all geodesics,
and projects under Ec â Ec to the geodesic flow. Therefore its completeness
is identical to the completeness of all geodesics. But r*A = AdgA for g  G0,
permuting the vector fields dual to all of the Ïa. The vector fields A for A  go are
always complete, moving up the fibers.
Remark 3. It is unknown whether there are manifolds of dimension greater than
one with all geodesics parabolic and closed, or with all geodesics elliptic and open.
6. PoSItIve RIccI CUrVaTurE
Theorem 6. Let M have a complete affine connection with Ricci curvature ten-
sor Ricij = 1 (Rkj + Rjki) positive definite, and bounded from below along any
COMPLETE PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS
geodesic by

for some constants c, â¬ > 0 (these constants possibly dependent on the choice of
geodesic), for all sufficiently large values of t, where t is the natural affine parameter
along a geodesic determined by the affine connection. Then the induced projective
connection on M is complete.
Proof. Pick coordinates xi along the geodesic, which we can assume (by parallel
transport of a frame) are constant on parallel transported vectors. There will be
infinitely many zeroes to solutions of the equation

where J and Y are Bessel functions. Kobayashi & Sasaki [12] proved that the
projective parameterizations are precisely the ratios u = (ay1 + b)/(cyo + d), for
y = yo, y = y1 any two linearly independent solutions of this equation, and a, b, c, d
any constants with ad  bc = 0. Therefore the projective parameterization wraps
around infinitely often, ensuring completeness of each geodesic by comparison to the
classification of projective connections on curves. Completeness of every geodesic
ensures completeness of the ambient projective connection by lemma 8 on the facing
page.
Remark 4. Every example known of such an affine connection is found on a
compact manifold; so practically this theorem only ensures the completeness of the
projective connections of compact Riemannian manifolds with positive Ricci cur-
vature. Kobayashi & Nagano [11] wonder whether projective completeness implies
compactness, which would imply a strengthened BonnetMyersCheng theorem, i.e.
that slower than quadratic Ricci curvature decay of a complete affine connection
would force compactness. This is not known even for Riemannian manifolds.
Remark 5. This theorem is unsettling, since it gives rise to many examples, but
depends on Ricci curvature, which is not an invariant of a projective connection.
We would like to find a criterion for completeness which can be checked in lots of
examples, and is projectively invariant. Moreover, it is unclear to what extent Ricci
curvature bounds are really required. In fact they are not: we will provide examples
of surfaces with projectively complete Riemannian metrics, whose curvature takes
on both positive and negative values.

Lemma 9. Suppose that a connected manifold M has a projective connection E â
M. Any infinitesimal symmetry of a projective connection E â M determines and
is determined by a unique vector field on M.

The sheaf of infinitesimal symmetries on C is the sheaf of solutions of a system
of linear ordinary differential equations, so by Picardâs theorem the local solutions
extend globally, never becoming multivalued because C is simply connected.
Lemma 11. C is complete just when every infinitesimal symmetry of the projective
connection on C is complete.
Proof. Follows from the classification of projective connections on curves.
Lemma 12. If C is complete then the map C â p1 preserves and reflects infini-
tesimal symmetries.
Proof. This is clear from the classification. Alternatively: the infinitesimal sym-
metries pullback to infinitesimal symmetries, because C â P1 is a covering map.
Infinitesimal symmetries comprise a 3 dimensional vector space, so they must match
precisely.

Lemma 13. If X is an infinitesimal symmetry, let
Then B(X) is a constant.
Proof. This is just the Killing form applied to X Î©â². It is also easy to check by
calculating the exterior derivative of B(X).
Definition 12. An infinitesimal symmetry X is called elliptic if B(X) < 0, para-
bolic if B(X) = 0 and X is not everywhere 0, and hyperbolic if B(X) > 0.
Lemma 14. Every elliptic infinitesimal symmetry has no zeros. Zeros of a hy-
perbolic infinitesimal symmetry (if there are any zeros) are of order 1. Zeros of a
parabolic infinitesimal symmetry (if there are any zeros) are of order precisely 2.
Proof. Zeros here mean on C, so equate upstairs on E to zeros of X1.
Either use the fact that this lemma holds true on P1, and local isomorphism of
sheaves of infinitesimal symmetries, or more simply note that at a zero:
B(X) =
which can't be negative, ruling out ellipticity. Moreover, it is positive (hyperbol-
icity) just when X1 â  0, i.e. just when dX1 = 0, a zero of order 1. Any zero of
order higher than 1 would ensure that the differential equations (3) for infinitesimal
symmetries have the same initial conditions as the 0 infinitesimal symmetry.
Lemma 15. C is complete just when all parabolic infinitesimal symmetries of C
are complete.
Proof. The infinitesimal symmetries form a Lie algebra spanned by the parabolic
ones. By Palais' theorem [15], a finite dimensional Lie algebra generated by com-
plete vector fields consists entirely of complete vector fields.
Alternatively, just look again at the classification of projective connections on
curves.
8. NORMAL PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS ON SURFACES
Definition 13. A projective connection is called normal if
0 = K ik
0 = Kil
and
0 = Kjkl + Kijk + Kiklj
Lemma 16 (Cartan [4]). Given a projective connection, there is a unique normal
projective connection with the same unparameterized geodesics.
We will prove this latter.
COMPLETE PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS
Lemma 17. Let M be a surface with normal projective connection E â M, and C
a periodic geodesic. If C has a nonzero Jacobi vector field, which vanishes at some
point of C, then C is a complete geodesic.
Proof. The equations of a Jacobi vector field are identical to the flat case, since
the relevant curvature vanishes. Therefore under development the Jacobi vector
fields are identified locally with Jacobi vector fields on the model. Any parabolic
infinitesimal symmetry on P1 has a zero between any two zeros of a Jacobi vector
field. But C  P1 is just an open interval, with the same differential system
for Jacobi vector fields, so the same is true on C. But this forces every parabolic
infinitesimal symmetry to have zeros arbitrarily far along C in both directions, since
the Jacobi vector fields have zeros in C, so periodically placed zeros in C arbitrarily
far along. This forces the parabolic infinitesimal symmetries to be complete, since
the flow of a parabolic infinitesimal symmetry will drive us toward its next zero.
9. TAMENESS
We follow LeBrun & Mason [13] closely here; keep in mind that their paper
treats projective structures, rather than the more general concept of projective
connection, so one has to check that the results quoted below hold, with the same
proofs, for arbitrary projective connections. Our aim is to show that every normal
projective connection on a surface whose geodesics are all closed has a nonzero
Jacobi vector field on each geodesic, i.e. lots of motions through closed geodesics.
Given a projective connection Ï : E â M, define a map â¡ : E â PTM by
requiring that for any geodesic C, e  EC â Î (e) = TÏ(e)C  PTM .
Lemma 18. This map â¡I is well-defined and smooth and identifies PT M = E/G+.
The foliation of E by the flow lines of the geodesic flow descends to a foliation of
PTM, whose leaves project to the geodesics in M.
Proof. To show that â¡I is well-defined, we have only to show that for each point
e  E, there is a geodesic C  M with e  EC. But we can just take the integral
manifold of the geodesic exterior differential system in E passing through e, and
it will project an appropriate geodesic C. Indeed the geodesic flow line through e
projects to an appropriate geodesic. This makes clear the smoothness of II.
Suppose that we have two points e0, e1  E with â¡ (e0) = â¡ (e1) =   PTM.
Then the integral manifolds Eco and Ec1 of the exterior differential system for
geodesics with ej  EC, must project to tangent geodesics: Tc0C0 = Tc,C1 for
some c0 and c1. The 1-forms Ï (ej) must therefore be linear multiples of one
another, which forces e0 and e1 to be in the same G+-orbits, by looking at how the
Ïi transform under right G+-action. Therefore EC = EC1 . So â¡ : E/G+ â PTM
is 1-1. Under right G0-action, rg0A = Adâ A ensuring that â¡I is onto, since this
action acts transitively on semibasic directions. To ensure that the inverse map
PTM â E/G+ is smooth, take any local section of E â E/G+, and attach to
each point   PT M the associated point of E; this is the point satisfying the
equations Ï1 (e) = 0 on l. Once again, examining the right action of G0, it is easy
to check that this point e is uniquely determined and smoothly so.
Definition 14. A projective connection E â M is called tame if the foliation of
PTM is locally trivial. It is called Zoll if all geodesics are embedded closed curves.
BENJAMIN MCKAY
Lemma 19 (LeBrun & Mason [13]). Any Zoll projective connection on a compact
surface is tame. The only surfaces which admit Zoll projective connections are the
sphere and the real projective plane.
Lemma 20 (LeBrun & Mason [13]). Let M be a surface bearing a Zoll projective
connection. The space of unoriented connected geodesics Î (i.e. the space of leaves
of the foliation of PT M ) is diffeomorphic to P2. The map PTM â Î is a smooth
fiber bundle, taking TcC â C (for C any geodesic and c  C ).
Lemma 21. Let Î be the space of geodesics of a tame Zoll projective connection
M. The map PTM â Î linearly identifies Jacobi vector fields with tangent vectors
to Î.
Proof. Every vector in TÎ gives rise to an infinitesimal motion through geodesics,
with nonvanishing normal velocity, and therefore by dimension count must account
for all of the Jacobi vector fields, since the ordinary differential equation for Jacobi
vector fields has well defined initial value problem.
Corollary 4. Zoll normal projective connections on surfaces are complete.
Example 5. Zoll [18] provides the following examples of Zoll metrics: for any odd
function f : [â1, 1] â (â1, 1), with f (z) = âf (âz) and f (â1) = f (1) = 0,

the other hand, if f(z) has large first derivative and small value at some point z0

f (z) = cos (xz) eâÎ±z2 sin (2 arctan (Î² (z â z0)) + 2 arctan (Î² (z + z0))) ,
with large constants Î± and Î², so that Î²eâÎ±zz is large, and any z0 â  0 with â1 <

Remark 6. LeBrun & Mason [13] provide explicit examples of Zoll affine connec-
tions on the sphere, which must also determine complete projective connections by
our results.
10. AFFINE CONNECTIONS, PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS, PROJECTIVE
STRUCTURES AND NORMAL PROJECTIVE CONNECTIONS
Just to clarify a few minor points in the literature, we would like to explain the
relations between affine connections, projective connections, projective structures

and arbitrary affine connections.

Corollary 5. The unparameterized geodesics of a projective connection are the
unparameterized geodesics of some affine connection.
Lemma 24. Given a projective connection, there is a torsion-free projective con-
nection (i.e. Kjk = 0) with the same geodesics, with the same projective parame-

Proof. Set Î³j = Î³j + 2 K jk.
Kobayashi [10] and Cartan [3] show that given a projective connection, there is a
unique normal projective connection with the same unparameterized geodesics. By
our result above, there are torsion-free connections with the same parameterized
geodesics as this normal projective connection. Two affine connections are said to
be projectively equivalent if they have the same unparameterized geodesics, and an
equivalence class is called a projective structure. Kobayashi [10] and Kobayashi &
Nagano [11] also show that given any torsion-free affine connection, there is a unique
bundle E â FM, whose sections are precisely the torsion-free affine connections,
and that this bundle bears a unique normal projective connection with the given
geodesics. Therefore a normal projective connection is essentially the same object
as a projective structure.

11. CONCLUSIONS
There is no clear common thread between our examples. Perhaps there is al-
ways a positive Ricci curvature affine connection for any Zoll normal projective
connection. Or, hoping for less, perhaps Zoll normal projective connections are
always complete for some other reason. It would be interesting to consider whether
Gromovâs h-principle methods would give topological conditions under which one
could produce a positive Ricci affine connection as a section of a normal projective
connection, so that one could have a topological criterion to force completeness of
all normal projective connections on some manifold. On the other hand, perhaps
any projective connection can be perturbed to an incomplete projective connection
without altering its unparameterized geodesics.
